request.query: Compare Docker Compose and Kubernetes for a small self-hosted stack

provider_rows.title: Docker Compose features and use cases
provider_rows.snippet: Docker Compose targets multi-container local and small-scale deployments with a single declarative file.

provider_rows.title: Kubernetes overview
provider_rows.snippet: Kubernetes automates deployment, scaling, and recovery for containerized workloads across clusters.

provider_rows.title: EKS cost optimization guidance
provider_rows.snippet: Managed Kubernetes adds operational overhead and cost controls that matter once workloads grow or require resilience.

pages.title: Docker Compose features and use cases
pages.content: Docker Compose lets operators define multi-container applications in a compose.yaml file, start the stack with a single command, and keep local or small self-hosted services simple. The guide focuses on developer workflows, quick iteration, and low operational overhead.

pages.title: Kubernetes overview
pages.content: Kubernetes manages desired state across a cluster, schedules containers, supports self-healing, rolling updates, and service discovery, and is intended for production workloads that need scaling and resilience. The overview also notes the steeper operational model compared with simpler tooling.

pages.title: EKS cost optimization guidance
pages.content: AWS documents that Kubernetes platforms introduce management and cost tradeoffs, including cluster overhead, autoscaling choices, and the need to right-size infrastructure. For smaller stacks, that overhead can outweigh the benefits unless high availability, scaling, or team workflows justify it.
